coredata.dc:title: Enhancing electrocatalytic performance and Stability: A novel Prussian Blue-Graphene quantum dot nanoarchitecture for H2O2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Chul Lim, Hong
Cho, Yujin
Han, Donghoon
Hyun Kim, Tae
coredata.dc:description: The reduction reaction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) stands as a pivotal electrochemical process holding the promise of revolutionizing energy conversion and water treatment applications. Realizing this potential hinges on the development of electrocatalysts that not only exhibit exceptional activity but also demonstrate unwavering stability. Herein, we propose a pioneering electrocatalyst composed of Prussian blue (PB)-graphene quantum dot (GQD) (PB-GQD) nanoarchitecture fabricated through an in-situ electrochemical method. The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture boasts precisely crafted PB nanoparticles (NPs) that exhibit remarkable electrochemical catalytic activity and stability, offering a substantial advancement over conventional PB NPs structure used as a control. The augmentation of electrocatalytic performance in the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture can be attributed to the symbiotic relationship between PB NPs and GQD. This synergy is engendered through electrostatic attraction and/or coordination interactions between PB precursor’s iron cations and GQD’s multivalent polyanions. This unique arrangement accelerates heterogeneous electron transport, endows the structure with abundant electrochemically active sites, and shortens mass transfer lengths. Notably, GQD’s presence, rich with hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxylic (–COOH) groups, mitigates the decomposition of PB NPs into Fe(OH)3 in neutral or higher pH solutions. This preservation mechanism ensures sustained, elevated electrocatalytic performance for H2O2 reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Prussian blue
Hydrogen peroxide
Nanoarchitecture
Electrocatalyst
Electrode active material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB
Electrochemical determination of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture toward H2O2
Preparation and Characterization of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture by chemical reduction method
Preparation and Characterization of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture by electrochemical deposition method
Enhanced electrocatalyst performance of PB-GQD toward H2O2 reduction reaction
Electrocatalysis Characterization of H2O2 determination at the PB-GQD/SPEs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), with the grant funded by Korea Government (MSIT and MOE) (No. NRF-2022R1F1A1064007 and NRF-2021R1A6A1A03039503). This research was supported by 'Regional Innovation Mega Project' program through the Korea Innovation Foundation funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (Project Number: 2023-DD-UP-0007). This research was also supported by “Regional Innovation Strategy (RIS)” through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in 2023(2022RIS-005). This work was also supported by the Soonchunhyang University Research Fund.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sequential change of FE-TEM images obtained during the formation of PB-GQD nanoarchitectures depending on (a) 3 h, (b) 12 h, (c) 24 h, and (d) 48 h after the addition of K-GQD in an aqueous solution containing FeCl3 and K3[Fe(CN)6] as PB precursors, (e) Elemental distribution of Fe, N, and C in the FE-TEM image.

Characterization of PB-GQD/ITO and PB/ITO. (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra. Inserted images are photograph of the electrodeposited PB-GQD/ITO. (b) XRD patterns. (c) and (d) FT-IR spectra, and XPS narrow spectra of (c) Fe 2p and (d) N 1s in PB-GQD, and (e) Fe 2p and (f) N 1s.
SEM images of (a, c) PB-GQD/ITO and (b, d) PB/ITO; confocal fluorescence lifetime-based images of (e,g) PB-GQD/ITO and (f, h) PB/ITO.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of PB-GQD/ITO in 0.01 M PBS (pH 4) solution with 0.1 M KCl at 50mV s−1. (b) Durability investigation of PB-GQD/SPEs. (c) Amperometric responses with successive addition of H2O2 in 0.01 M PBS (pH 4) buffer solution containing 0.1 M KCl at the PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs with a working potential of 0.00 V (vs Ag/AgCl). (d) The effect of the pH for 50 μM H2O2 at the PB-GQD/SPE and PB/SPE with a working potential of 0.00 V (vs Ag/AgCl).

Amperometric responses of (a) PB-GQD/SPEs and (b) PB/SPEs to successive additions of H2O2 in 0.01 M PBS (pH 4) buffer solution containing 0.1 M KCl with a working potential of 0.00 V (vs Ag/AgCl). Corresponding calibration curves for H2O2 determination at (c) PB-GQD/SPEs and (d) PB/SPEs based electrochemical biosensors.
(a) Reproducibility measurement of PB-GQD/SPE for H2O2 determination in 5 samples. (b) Chronoamperometric response to H2O2 in the presence of interfering species.
Schematic diagram of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture formation through electrochemical deposition process.

head.ce:title: Enhancing electrocatalytic performance and Stability: A novel Prussian Blue-Graphene quantum dot nanoarchitecture for H2O2 reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Sangji University, Wonju 26339, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Sangji University, Wonju 26339, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Catholic University of Korea, Bucheon, Gyeonggi-do 14662, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Catholic University of Korea, Bucheon, Gyeonggi-do 14662, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture shows high electrochemical catalytic activity and stability for H2O2 reduction reaction.

The reduction reaction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) stands as a pivotal electrochemical process holding the promise of revolutionizing energy conversion and water treatment applications. Realizing this potential hinges on the development of electrocatalysts that not only exhibit exceptional activity but also demonstrate unwavering stability. Herein, we propose a pioneering electrocatalyst composed of Prussian blue (PB)-graphene quantum dot (GQD) (PB-GQD) nanoarchitecture fabricated through an in-situ electrochemical method. The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture boasts precisely crafted PB nanoparticles (NPs) that exhibit remarkable electrochemical catalytic activity and stability, offering a substantial advancement over conventional PB NPs structure used as a control. The augmentation of electrocatalytic performance in the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture can be attributed to the symbiotic relationship between PB NPs and GQD. This synergy is engendered through electrostatic attraction and/or coordination interactions between PB precursor’s iron cations and GQD’s multivalent polyanions. This unique arrangement accelerates heterogeneous electron transport, endows the structure with abundant electrochemically active sites, and shortens mass transfer lengths. Notably, GQD’s presence, rich with hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxylic (–COOH) groups, mitigates the decomposition of PB NPs into Fe(OH)3 in neutral or higher pH solutions. This preservation mechanism ensures sustained, elevated electrocatalytic performance for H2O2 reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalysts for the redox reactions of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a versatile intermediate in biological and environmental processes, are attracting increasing attention.[1,2] H2O2, a reactive oxygen species, is one of the crucial biomarkers associated with oxidative stress involving cell growth, immune activation, and apoptosis in the field of clinical diagnostics. Accordingly, various electrochemical biosensors have been developed to quantitatively determine H2O2 concentration in the human body for disease diagnosis related to diabetes, atherosclerosis, renal disorders, cancer, and aging.[3] H2O2 is also a powerful oxidant that can effectively degrade various recalcitrant organic compounds into harmless byproducts such as carbon dioxide, water, and mineralized acids in advanced oxidation process.[4] This is because H2O2 is converted into a hydroxyl radical (hydroxyl, •OH) with strong oxidizing power Eo (•OH/H2O) = 2.8 V, NHE) and low selectivity through spontaneous redox reactions.[5] Thus, in order to realize a high electrocatalytic activity for H2O2 reduction reaction, the electrocatalysts must have a fast, reversible, and stable redox reaction. One promising candidate as an electrocatalyst for H2O2 reduction is Prussian Blue (PB), which consists of a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure with alternating Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions coordinated to carbon and nitrogen atoms by cyanide groups, respectively in the unit cell.[6] PB, a mixed-valent iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II) complex, is considered an artificial peroxidase due to its reversible redox behaviors, high electrocatalytic activity, and low redox potential.[7] However, there are unresolved challenges that significantly restrict the use of PB as an electrocatalyst for H2O2 reduction. Firstly, during the redox reactions of PB, penetration of H2O2 and counter ions into the inside of PB structure causes volume changes and mechanical stress in the PB structure consequently leading to a reduction in the stability of PB.[8] Secondly, the inferior electrical conductivity of PB leads to undesirable side reactions that degrade electrochemical stability of PB.[9,10] Thirdly, the decomposition of PB occurs under solution conditions with a pH of neutral or higher.[11].

To overcome these challenges, various PB analogs (PBAs) have been developed by incorporating zero-, one-, and two-dimensional materials to improve the electrochemical activity and stability of PB.[12] Among PBAs, core@shell nanocomposites are composed of electrical conducting polymers, noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), and metal oxide-wrapped PB NPs, where the shell protects the PB core to ensure high stability while also connecting the PB NPs to improve electron transfer.[13–15] The challenge of the core@shell structure is that the inherent electrocatalytic activity of PB deteriorates due to the encapsulation of PB. An alternative approach is to form PB NPs on a substrate such as graphene, carbon nanotube, and MoS2 nanosheet.[16,17] However, achieving the uniform formation of PB NPs on these substrate materials is challenging, and there is still a drawback of compromised stability of PB NPs in solutions with neutral or higher pH conditions. In addition, employing polymer binders such as polyvinylidene fluoride, polystyrene, or Nafion to affix chemically synthesized PBAs on electrode surfaces introduces further complexity. It becomes still challenging to regulate morphology and attain the desired electrical and electrochemical activity of PBAs.[17,18] Therefore, in order to achieve high-efficiency H2O2 reduction while solving these problems, it is essential to develop electrocatalysts that are simple in synthesis, electrochemically stable, and cost-effective.

In this paper, we demonstrated a novel class of nanoarchitecture consisting of PB and graphene quantum dot (GQD) (PB-GQD) with enhanced electrochemical catalytic activity and stability toward the H2O2 reduction reaction. The GQD assumes a pivotal role by serving as a substrate that not only fosters the growth of PB NPs with precision and uniformity but also triggers this growth through coordination interactions and/or electrostatic attractions with the iron cations—an integral precursor for PB. Consequently, the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture exhibits abundant electrochemically active sites and short mass transfer length, which effectively mitigates strain caused by volume changes, thereby minimizing the loss of PB NPs during redox reaction of PB. Moreover, the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture effectively mitigates the hydrolysis of PB NPs even in neutral or higher pH solutions. This is accomplished through acid-base reactions involving oxygen-containing functional groups—such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) entities located at the peripheries of GQD—alongside OH− ions present within the reaction milieu. This harmonious interplay sustains an elevated electrocatalytic performance for the H2O2 reduction reaction, further solidifying the prowess of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture. Moreover, the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture can be easily fabricated by an in-situ electrochemical method, which makes it a promising candidate for electrode active materials for various electrochemical applications such as sodium‐ion batteries, electrochemical biosensors, and electrochromic devices.

All the chemical reagents used in this study were commercially available and used without further purification. Carbon nanofiber, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), dopamine hydrochloride, uric acid, phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4, 0.1 M), D-(+)-glucose, L-cysteine, potassium nitrae (KNO3), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol, and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Daejung Chemical & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea). Screen-printed electrodes (working electrode: 4 mm diameter Pt, counter electrode: Pt, and reference electrode: Ag) were purchased from Metrohm DropSens (Metrohm AG, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB
ce:section.ce:para: The potassium-graphene quantum dot (K-GQD) ionic complex utilized in this work was prepared by hydrothermal oxidation of carbon nanofibers, according to the procedure described in our previous reports.[19] The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB on indium tin oxide (ITO, 13 ohm sq−1) and screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) were prepared by electrochemical method using chronoamperometry. For the preparation of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB, a total of 5 cycles were carried out, ranging from 0.72 to 0.77 V over a duration of 250 s and 150 s for SPEs and ITO electrodes, respectively. This electrochemical process took place either using SPEs or within a three-electrode system comprising a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), a counter electrode (Pt wire), and a working electrode (ITO) in an aqueous solution containing 0.05 M K3Fe(CN)6, 0.05 M FeCl3·6H2O, and K-GQD. After electrochemical processing, all samples were rinsed with DI water dried in oven at 100 °C for 10 min.

The electrochemical behaviors of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB electrodeposited on substrates such as ITO and SPEs were evaluated in 0.01 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl under a three-electrode system (reference electrode: Ag/AgCl, counter electrode: Pt wire, and working electrode: PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB). The electron transfer kinetics (k o) was determined from ΔEp of 100 mV s−1 using Nicholson’s method. All electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature without removing oxygen from the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture toward H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical determination of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB toward H2O2 was performed by chronoamperometry in 0.01 M PBS (pH 4.0) buffer solution containing 0.1 M KCl with a working potential of 0.00 V (vs Ag/AgCl).

The electrochemical experiments were performed with a CH instrument (electrochemical analyzer, CHI 660D (CH Instruments, Inc., Austin, TX, USA, Z-202306208148 at the Research Support Center for Bio-Bigdata Analysis and Utilization of Biological Resources) using a three-electrode system at room temperature. The optical properties of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB were measured using ultraviolet–visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) absorption spectrometer (JASCO, V-670, Hachioji, Tokyo). The chemical composition of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos, AXIS Supra) using a monochromatic Alkα (15 kV, 375 W). Field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) (JEOL, JEM-F200) was performed at 200 kV for the acquisition of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB NPs images. The surface morphology of the electrodeposited thin films was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, S-4300). The crystal structures of PB-GQD and PB were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker, D8 Advance) using Cukα1-1.5418 Å (40 kV, 40 mA). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Vertex80v (Bruker, USA) to investigate functional groups of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture, PB, and K-GQD. Thirty-two accumulative scans were collected from 600 to 4000 cm−1. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) (Xenocs, Xeuss2.0) experiments were carried out to clarify the size and dispersion state of nanoparticles. 2D SAXS images from each sample were recorded for an x-ray wavelength of 1.5 Å and a sample to detector distance of 2,500 mm (collimation-HR). Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) experiments were performed with MicroTime 100 (PicoQuant GmbH, Germany), a BX41 upright microscope (Olympus). A 40x, 0.65NA Air objective Plan N (Olympus) was equipped with a high-precision XY scanner (P-733.2CD, Physik Instrumente GmbH, Germany) controlled by a digital multi-channel piezo controller (E-727, Physik Instrumente). Photons were counted using a MultiHarp 150 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) unit (PicoQuant GmbH) and imaged with a single photon avalanche photodiode (SPAD; SPCM-AQRH-14-TR, Excelitas Technologies, Canada) using a 473 nm long-pass edge filter (Semrock, US). The TCSPC equipment consisted of a 470 nm pulsed diode laser (LDH-P-C-470, PicoQuant GmbH) with a PDL 820 “Sepia II” (PicoQuant GmbH). Calculations for FLIM and time gating were conducted by SymPhoTime 64 software. The FLIM imaged were taken in 256 ⅹ 256 pixels with a pixel size of 0.313 µm ⅹ 0.313 µm, making an image of 80 µm ⅹ 80 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture by chemical reduction method
ce:section.ce:para: Upon the introduction of K-GQD to the solution containing FeCl3 and K3[Fe(CN)6] the precursor components for PB a remarkable transition in color from red-orange to green was observed. This alteration was accompanied by visible signs of precipitation after allowing the mixture to rest at room temperature for a day. In contrast, no color change or precipitate formation was observed in the solution containing only FeCl3 and K3[Fe(CN)6] even after several days, as illustrated in Fig. S1a. The color change and subsequent precipitation are attributed to the formation of PB NPs on the basal plane and edges of the GQDs. This intricate process unfolds through a direct reaction involving the multivalent GQD polyanions and the dissociated Fe3+ ions from FeCl3 and Fe(CN)6 3−, both functioning as PB precursors. It is noteworthy that the K-GQD is a nano-sized ionic complex formed by electrostatic attraction between K+ ions and carboxylic groups present on the edges of GQDs. Consequently, GQDs bearing carbonyl groups (–COO−) can generate electrostatic attraction and/or coordination interactions with Fe3+ ions.[20–22] This phenomenon results in the reduction of Fe(CN)6 3− ions to Fe(CN)6 2− ions by the multivalent GQD polyanions, followed by a subsequent reaction with Fe3+ to form PB NPs in the solution. The formation of PB-GQD nanoarchitectures was observed by a time-dependent FE-TEM and EDS study from PB NPs co-aggregated with GQDs in the aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 1 . Furthermore, the shape of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture formed from the reaction between FeCl3, K3[Fe(CN)6], and K-GQD was distinctly different from that of the PB NPs made solely from FeCl3 and K3[Fe(CN)6], as shown in Fig. S1b and c. As the reaction time increased, the size of the PB seeds grown on the basal plane and edge of GQDs expanded and the distance between them decreased. This allowed them to easily aggregate into the PB-GQD nanoarchitectures. The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture was a deep blue powder with an absorption spectrum 700 nm, related to the intervalence transition (metal–ligand-metal) between Fe2+ and Fe3+, as shown in Figure S2.[23] Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of K-GQD suspensions with and without PB NPs were examined. The K-GQD displayed an excitation-dependent PL emission spectrum that shifted from blue to red with decreasing excitation energy. Blue emission is associated with the quantum confinement effect from the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms within the structure of GQDs, while redshift results from the edge effects due to oxygen functional groups at the edge sites of GQDs [19], as depicted in Figure S3. These results confirm the formation of PB-GQD nanostructures, which agrees with FE-TEM and EDS analyses. Consequently, it becomes evident that K-GQD assumes a dual role simultaneously operating as a supporting electrolyte and a substrate, offering essential nucleation sites for the creation of PB NPs through the electrochemical deposition process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of PB-GQD nanoarchitecture by electrochemical deposition method
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible reaction mechanism for the formation of PB-GQD nanostructure on the surface of electrode substrates (e.g., ITO and SPE) with a potential of 0.75 V (Ag/AgCl) is described in Scheme 1 . Initially, in the electrochemical cell environment, Fe(CN)6 3− ions experience migration, resulting in either electrostatic attraction or coordination bonding interactions with multivalent GQD polyanions located on the electrode surface. Subsequently, Fe(CN)6 3− ions undergo reduction through heterogeneous electron transfer by the applied potential of 0.75 V (Ag/AgCl) through GQDs located on the electrode surface. In the third step, a robust ionic/covalent bond is formed between Fe3+ ions dissociated from FeCl3 and reduced Fe(CN)6 4− species, driven by the principles of hard and soft acids and bases theory.[24] As a result, the interaction between Fe3+ ions and reduced Fe(CN)6 4− ions triggers the formation of Fe(III)-N-C-Fe(II) complexes at both the basal plane and the edges of the GQD. This process culminates in the emergence of thin films featuring electrodeposited, blue-colored PB-GQD nanoarchitecture on the surface of ITO, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 2 a. The UV–Vis spectrum of PB-GQD/ITO shows a broad absorption peak around 700 nm (Fig. 2a), which is attributed to the metal–ligand-metal charge transfer between Fe2+ and Fe3+. This charge transfer is responsible for the blue color observed in PB-GQD. The same peak is also observed in the spectrum of PB-GQD nanoarchitectures obtained from the precipitation reaction, confirming the formation of PB-GQD. In contrast, the control sample PB/ITO shows an additional peak around 400 nm, which is due to the overlapping of the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) band of Fe(CN)6 3−.[25] The XRD analysis confirms the crystal structures of both thin films. Their XRD patterns, as shown in Fig. 2b, are almost identical,indicating that they have the same FCC crystal structure. These patterns exhibit discernible peaks at approximately 17.3°, 24.7°, 35.2°, 39.5°, 43.4°, and 55.0°, corresponding to the (200), (220), (400), (420), (422), and (600) planes of FCC structure.[26] SAXS measurements were also performed to clarify the size and dispersion state of the PB NPs. The peak intensity of PB-GQD/ITO shows strong peaks at q z = 0.206, 0.273, and 0.318 nm−1, whereas that of PB/ITO (q z = 0.226 and 0.276 nm−1) exhibits a weak peak, as shown in Fig. S4a. Furthermore, the evolution of 2D image corresponding 1D-WAXD intensity profile was clearly observed from PB-GQD/ITO, compared to PB/ITO, as depicted in Fig. S4b and c. As a result, the increased peak intensity and sharper 2D image in PB-GQD/ITO indicate higher crystallinity and larger particle size of PB NPs compared to those in PB/ITO. The FT-IR spectra of PB-GQD and PB show the characteristic–C≡N– stretching vibration at around 2067 cm−1, which is a fingerprint of PB.[27] Moreover, spectral bands at around 3062 cm−1 and at 1605 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of the –OH groups.[26] These groups are associated with absorbed water molecules within the PB structure and/or the –OH groups present on GQDs in PB-GQD, as depicted in Fig. 2c. The magnified FT-IR spectra of PB-GQD/ITO and PB/ITO show that only PB-GQD/ITO has peaks at around 1760 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1, which are related to the C = O stretching and O–H bending of the carboxyl groups on GQDs within PB-GQD,[27] as shown in Fig. 2d. The chemical composition of PB-GQD and PB was also investigated by XPS, as shown in Fig. 2e–h. The higher-resolution spectra of Fe 2p reveal distinctive peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, corresponding to high spin–Fe and low spin–Fe in the PB structure, indicative of the redox reactivity of PB.[27] In both samples, the Fe3+ species associated with high spin–Fe exhibit feature peak sat around 709.5 and 722.6 eV for Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. As for Fe2+ species related to low spin–Fe, four characteristic peaks emerge at approximately 708.2, 712.3, 721.1, and 725.2 eV, respectively.[27] Notably, three major peaks around 397.5 eV, 399.1 eV, and 402.0 eV correspond to the N 1s main peaks, attributable to Fe–C≡N within the PB structure. The atomic ratios of ratio FeII and FeIII calculated by XPS are similar for both samples. Collectively, the results from XRD, XPS, and FT-IR converge to validate the successful electrodeposition of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture through an in-situ electrochemical deposition process involving the components of K-GQD and PB precursors.

The alterations in the morphology of the electrodeposited thin films (PB-GQD/ITO and PB/ITO) can exert an influence on the mass transport dynamics within the film and modulate the rate of interfacial electron transfer.[28] Fig. 3 a-d show the SEM images of PB-GQD/ITO and PB/ITO. PB-GQD/ITO has a smooth surface, while PB/ITO has an aggregated morphology with several hundred nanometers in size. To discern the distribution of PB NPs, we employed time-resolved confocal fluorescence spectroscopy, analyzing the fluorescent imagery generated by PB NPs. Directly imaging the distribution of PB NPs within thin films electrodeposited on the electrode surface is crucial for understanding electrochemical reactions occurring at the surface from the perspective of electrocatalytic activity. We scanned a localized laser excitation (λex = 470 nm) over the region of interest on PB-GQD/ITO and PB/ITO, and obtained the FLIM of PB NPs, as shown in Fig. 3e-h. The FLIM results show that the lifetime of PB NPs is 6.62 ns, which was obtained from the histogram corresponding to the fluorescence lifetime imaging of PB NPs within the electrodeposited thin films, as shown in Figure S5. The noteworthy aspect is that PB NPs within PB-GQD/ITO exhibit a more compact and uniform distribution compared to those within PB/ITO. It is worth noting that fluorescence patterns are very similar to the distribution of small and bright spots observed in the SEM images (Fig. 3c and d). These outcomes collectively illustrate that the growth mechanism of PB is influenced by the presence of GQDs during the electrochemical deposition process. This influence contributes to a higher density of electrochemical catalytic active sites, which are primed for engagement in the electrochemical reaction involving H2O2 (as elaborated in the section titled “Enhanced Electrocatalyst Performance of PB-GQD towards H2O2 Reduction Reaction”). The lifetime of PB NPs is 6.62 ns, which was obtained from the histogram corresponding to the FLIM of PB NPs within the electrodeposited thin film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced electrocatalyst performance of PB-GQD toward H2O2 reduction reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical behaviors of PB-GQD/ITO were evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 0.01 M PBS (pH 4) solution with 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, as depicted in Fig. 4 a. The resulting CV curve shows two well-defined redox couples, with half-wave potentials (E1/2 = (Epa + Epc)/2) of 0.07 and 0.80 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for the redox couples I and II, respectively. The redox couple I, spanning from 0.3 to −0.15 V, corresponds to Fe3+ coordinated to nitrogen, signifying the conversion of PB to Prussian white (PW) which could be written as the reaction (1).[29,30]
(1)

4
III
Fe
II
C N
6
3
+ 4
K

+ 4
e
-
⇄
K 4
Fe
4
II
Fe
II
C N
6
3
PB (coloration state) → → PW (bleaching state)

The peak potential separation (ΔEp ) of the redox couple I is 0.15 V. The redox couple II between 0.7 and 1.1 V is associated with the low spin state of Fe2+ coordinated to carbon (the transition of PB and Berlin green (BG)). This can be written as the reaction (2).
(2)
Fe
4
III
Fe
II
C N
6
3
+ 4
Cl
-
⇄
K 4
Fe
4
III
Fe
III
C N
6
C l
3
+ 3
e
-
PB (coloration state) → BG (coloration state)

ΔEp value of redox couple II is 0.14 V. The redox reaction of PB-GQD entails the intercalation and deintercalation of K+ ions within the lattice channels of PB to maintain charge balance, which is related to the electrochemically active surface area and charge transfer kinetics of PB NPs. To gain insights into the electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2, a comprehensive investigation of the redox reactions of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs in the redox couple I was carried out at various scan rates. The peak currents displayed a linear relationship with the square root of the scan rate, indicating the diffusion-controlled nature of the heterogeneous electron transfer reaction.[19,31] The obtained results are depicted in Figure S6. Despite a slightly diminished Faraday current in PB-GQD/SPEs compared to PB/SPEs, the PB-GQD/SPEs exhibited heightened reversibility in reactions with a narrower ΔEp value and a ratio of peak current (ipa/ipc) closer to 1, in contrast to PB/SPEs, at all scan rates. Moreover, the reduction reaction of PB-GQD/SPEs observed at a less negative potential than that of PB/SPEs. These results suggest that PB-GQD has a fast heterogeneous electron transfer rate (k o), leading to a decrease in overpotential during redox reactions. The k o values of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs were calculated with K3[Fe(CN)6] according to Nicholson’s method as equation (1).[32]
(1)
ψ
=
k
o
π
D
o
v
(
F
R
T
) ]
= f
( Δ
E P
)
where D o is diffusion coefficient of Fe(CN)6 3− (7.63 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), T is 298 K, R is the gas constant, F is the Faraday constant, and the dimensionless rate parameter ψ was estimated from ΔEp when scan rate (v) is 0.1 V s−1.[33] The calculated k o value of PB-GQD/SPEs (k o = 1.97 × 10-3 cm s−1) was 4 times higher than that of PB-SPEs (k o = 0.51 × 10−3 cm s−1). This, along with the less reduction potential, narrow ΔEp , and fast k o value suggests that GQDs in the PB-GQD significantly enhanced the electrochemical catalytic activity of PB NPs. The PB-GQD/SPEs showed superior cycling stability compared to PB/SPEs over a potential range of −0.15 to +1.15 V in a 0.01 M PBS solution (pH 4) containing 0.1 M KCl with a scan rate of 0.05 V, as summarized in Table S1 and Figure S7. While PB/SPEs exhibited a substantial decline of 42% in the reduction peak current compared to its initial value after 100 cycles, PB-GQD/SPEs demonstrated a notably minor loss of initial activity, limited to only 17%. Moreover, compared to PB/SPE, PB-GQD/SPE had a 2-fold higher reduction peak current and a 40 mV positive shift in the reduction peak potential. These results imply that PB-GQD has high electrochemical catalytic activity and durability during redox reactions. Remarkably, PB-GQD/SPEs exhibited exceptional stability even after 2000 cycles in the potential range of −0.3 to +0.3 V without any discernible change in peak current and peak potential, as shown in Fig. 4b. These results indicate that PB-GQD effectively suppresses changes in the lattice structure triggered by the redox interaction between K+ ions and FeIII located in PB lattice, thereby maintaining completely the catalytic activity of PB NPs. Chronoamperometry method was employed to investigate the reduction reaction of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs. This is because the reduction reaction of PB NPs associated with the high-spin iron signal exhibits high voltammetric responses, while the oxidation reaction related to the low-spin iron signal is kinetically slow as well as low electrochemical stability.[34,35] The electrochemical catalytic behavior of PB-GQDs/SPEs and PB/SPEs for H2O2 reduction were explored by continuously adding 50 μM H2O2 at a working potential of 0.00 V (Ag/AgCl) in a 0.01 M PBS (pH 4) buffer solution using amperometric responses. In this process, PB is initially reduced to PW, and subsequently, the PW species promotes the reduction of H2O2 to regenerate PB. These results indicate that PW undergoes electron exchange with H2O2 through a cycle-mediated redox process, in line with the EC’ catalytic model.[36] As the concentration of added H2O2 increased, the amperometric curves of PB-GQD/SPEs exhibited notably faster response rates and higher response currents compared to PB/SPEs, as shown in Fig. 4c. The response times of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs, defined as the time required to attain 90% of the steady-state current after adding H2O2, were 1.5 s and 3.1 s, respectively. This improved electrochemical catalytic activity of PB-GQD/SPEs can be attributed to the role of GQD in facilitating enhanced heterogeneous electron transfer and high electrochemical surface area in PB-GQD nanoarchitecture. The effect of solution pH on the electrocatalytic reaction of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs to H2O2 reduction was also investigated. It is known that PB is susceptible to the formation of iron hydroxide (Fe(OH)3) with increasing pH due to the reaction between FeIII in PB and OH− ions. This leads to the decomposition of PB into soluble substances such as ferrocyanide (FeII(CN)6 4−) and Fe3+ ions, thereby diminishing its electrochemical catalytic activity.[37,38] The amperometric response values were plotted at various pH conditions ranging from pH 3 to pH 9 in 0.01 M PBS buffer solution containing 0.1 M KCl, as depicted in Fig. 4d. Although the electrocatalytic reaction with H2O2 diminished with increasing pH for both samples, PB-GQD/SPEs demonstrated notably greater stability under neutral or higher pH conditions than PB/SPEs. The remarkable electrochemical stability of PB-GQD/SPEs underscores that the intercalation/deintercalation of potassium cations (K+) and the presence of anions such as hydroxide (OH−) and chloride (Cl−) have minimal impact on the integrity of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture during its redox reactions. The enhanced electrochemical catalytic activity and stability of PB-GQD/SPEs be explained by the following reasons: (1) The PB NPs grown on the basal plane and edges of GQD would effectively prevent the loss of PB NPs due to the enhanced electrostatic attraction and(or) coordination interaction between multivalent GQD polyanions and iron ions of PB;[35,39] (2) The oxygen-containing functional groups on the edges of GQD could effectively suppress the hydrolysis of ferric ions, thereby minimizing the loss of PB NPs and enhancing the redox reaction stability of PB, as illustrated in Scheme 1; (3) Smaller and uniformly dispersed PB NPs would reduce the strain caused by volume changes and provide shorter diffusion lengths for the potassium ions during redox reactions of PB.[31,40] Notably, the morphological distinction between these two samples is related to the size and dispersion of PB NPs in the thin film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalysis Characterization of H2O2 determination at the PB-GQD/SPEs
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of detection (LOD) and dynamic range were investigated by measuring the amperometric response of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs to the successive addition of 0.1 mM – 100 mM H2O2 with a working potential of 0.00 V (vs Ag/AgCl) for the application of electrochemical biosensors, as depicted in Fig. 5 a and b. The selection of a working electrode potential of 0.00 V in the H2O2 reduction reaction is crucial in the field of electrochemical biosensor applications due to its ability to mitigate interferences from other substances through low overpotential. The plot correlating the reduction peak current with H2O2 concentration exhibited linearity with correlation coefficients of 0.9933 (PB-GQD/SPEs) and 0.9862 (PB/SPEs) for 10 ∼ 2000 μM and 50 ∼ 4000 μM, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 5c and d. The LOD of PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs was estimated to be 4.39 and 34.5 uM, respectively based on the criterion of a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (insets in Fig. 5c and d). The electrochemical catalytic response of PB-GQD/SPEs to H2O2 determination exhibited strong reproducibility across all five electrodes, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The selectivity of PB-GQD/SPEs was studied by measuring the amperometric response to the H2O2 reduction reaction in the presence of several electrochemical interfering species at a fixed potential of 0.00 V (Ag/AgCl). These interfering species, including DA, UA, glucose, L-cysteine, NO3 −, and K+ ions, can potentially hinder the electrochemical signals, affecting both selectivity and reactivity towards H2O2. However, the addition and presence of these interfering species did not induce significant changes in the response current for H2O2 determination, as depicted in Fig. 6b. This underscores the lack of impact from interfering species on the accuracy of H2O2 determination. Comparatively, most prior research efforts have concentrated on PB-based composite materials. In contrast, the PB-GQD/SPEs developed within this study exclusively leverage the electrochemical catalytic activity of H2O2 reduction facilitated solely by PB. This strategy yields notably enhanced performance metrics in terms of the linear response range, sensitivity, and detection limit. A concise summary of the analytical results can be found in Table S2.

The PB-GQD nanoarchitecture demonstrates remarkable efficacy in the H2O2 reduction reaction, driven by its fast heterogeneous electron transport, abundant electrochemically active sites, and efficient mass transfer capabilities. Compared to the PB control, the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture boasts a fourfold higher k o value, underpinning its capacity for reversible and stable redox reactions characterized by narrower ΔE
p values and reduced redox peak potentials. In addition, the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture effectively suppresses the hydrolysis of ferric ions, exhibiting superior electrochemical stability even in neutral or higher pH conditions. This results in sustained and robust electrocatalytic performance for the H2O2 reduction reaction. The exceptional electrocatalytic performance of the PB-GQD nanoarchitecture owes its origin to the judicious incorporation of small, uniformly distributed PB NPs cultivated on the basal plane and edges of GQD. This growth is facilitated through electrostatic attraction and/or coordination interactions between the iron cations of the PB precursor and the multivalent GQD polyanions. These findings affirm the viability of integrating GQDs with PB NPs to establish a nanoarchitecture, thereby presenting a promising avenue to elevate the electrochemical catalytic activity of PB NPs. Our work on this novel PB-GQD nanostructure stands as a milestone in the exploration of pioneering electrocatalysts and electrode active materials for the evolution of next-generation electrochemical devices.

Hong Chul Lim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Yujin Cho: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Donghoon Han: Data curation, Supervision. Tae Hyun Kim: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), with the grant funded by Korea Government (MSIT and MOE) (No. NRF-2022R1F1A1064007 and NRF-2021R1A6A1A03039503). This research was supported by 'Regional Innovation Mega Project' program through the Korea Innovation Foundation funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (Project Number: 2023-DD-UP-0007). This research was also supported by “Regional Innovation Strategy (RIS)” through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in 2023(2022RIS-005). This work was also supported by the Soonchunhyang University Research Fund.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Comparison of photograph and TEM images for PB-GQD nanoarchitecture and PB NPs; UV–Vis absorption spectra; comparison of CV data for electrodeposited PB-GQD/SPEs and PB/SPEs; comparison of electrochemical parameters; comparison of electrochemical sensing properties for determination H2O2 with some reported works. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158920.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Gong, New nanocomposite based on Prussian blue nanoparticles/Carbon nanotubes/Chitosan and its application for assembling of amperometric glucose biosensor, Anal. Lett. 39 (2006) 913–926, https://doi.org/10.1080/00032710600614057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prussian blue based screen printed biosensors with improved characteristics of long-term lifetime and pH stability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ricci, A. Amine, G. Palleschi, D. Moscone, Prussian blue based screen printed biosensors with improved characteristics of long-term lifetime and pH stability, Biosens. Bioelectron. 18 (2003) 165–174, https://doi.org/10.1016/S0956-5663(02)00169-0.
